title: Authority Governance Model — Approval Gates, Delegation, Break-Glass, Human Override
context: Tribunus needs structured authority governance to manage risk across unattended agent execution, human-in-the-loop decisions, emergency situations, and delegation chains. Without explicit governance contracts, every subsystem invents its own approval flow, delegation becomes permanent by default, emergencies have no safe path, and human overrides may bypass governance rather than augment it.
decision:
Define four governance mechanisms as a unified model.

APPROVAL AND COUNCIL GATES:
- Four approval strategies: single (one approver), council (configurable quorum — fraction or absolute count), automatic (low-risk, receipt-only), timeout (escalate if not approved within window).
- Council quorum is configurable per gate. Default: majority of council members.
- Veto powers are assigned to specific roles (design-council-chair, security-lead, release-manager). A veto forces the approval back to review.
- Timeout escalation promotes a pending approval to the next authority tier: single → council, council → break-glass, break-glass → human-override.
- Automatic approval for classified-low-risk actions: reads within scope, known-safe tools, pre-approved mutation paths. All auto-approvals produce receipts.

DELEGATION EXPIRY AND REVOCATION:
- Every delegation has a mandatory TTL. No permanent delegation without renewal.
- Expired delegations cannot be used for authority decisions — the PDP rejects them.
- Revocation propagates immediately: all derived capabilities, cached decisions, and transitive delegations are invalidated.
- Cascading revocation walks the delegation chain and revokes every descendant.
- Revocation events are recorded in the evidence chain (RecoveryReceiptTable) with the revoker identity, timestamp, and reason.

BREAK-GLASS AUTHORITY:
- Break-glass override can bypass normal policy evaluation for emergency scenarios (system recovery, security incident, data integrity emergency).
- Every break-glass event records: the actor identity, the action taken, the timestamp, and a mandatory reason.
- Break-glass authority is time-boxed: a configurable emergency window (default 1 hour) after which authority auto-revokes.
- Post-hoc review is mandatory within a configurable SLA (default 24 hours).
- Failure to complete post-hoc review escalates to the council for investigation.

HUMAN OVERRIDE SEMANTICS:
- Human override is a distinct authority action type with its own policy tier above agent decisions.
- Override records capture: the original agent decision, the overriding human identity, the reason for override, and the timestamp.
- Overrides are subject to periodic review based on risk level: low-risk quarterly, medium-risk monthly, high-risk within 24 hours.
- Overrides can be reversed by subsequent authority decisions (human or council).
- Agent-initiated operations yield to human override within defined authority boundaries — an agent cannot overrule a human override.
consequences: All authority decisions flow through a structured governance pipeline. No ad-hoc approvals. No permanent delegations without renewal. No emergency without audit trail. No human override without periodic review. The authority vocabulary (principal, actor, delegate, capability, grant, decision, approval, revocation, escalation) provides the type system for these governance mechanisms. The PDP/PEP architecture (ADR 0024) enforces these rules at every authority boundary.
